text:
Two great points:
* It's going to be very hard to weed out spurious AI-generated books from the real ones.
* If too much of the training data is itself AI-generated…the large language models at the heart of AI systems will deteriorate or "collapse."

text:
model collapse: a learning process where models forget improbable events and converge to a single estimate

tl;dr - we still need human-generated content to maintain the benefits of large-scale data training.

or maybe we leave it as an exercise for the real world😉

arxiv.org/abs/2305.17493

What happens to machine learning models when they feed on themselves, when the data they ingest comes more and more from other generative models rather than human authors?

This concept of Model Collapse and much more in this interview with expert Ilia Shumailov: www.theregister.com/2024/01/26/w...

There's also a BIG reason why they're trying to use social media sites to get more training data. Because model collapse is a thing 

www.theatlantic.com/technology/a...

text: The actual figure here is not 99%, and there are special kinds of caution we may want to apply to nonfiction. But there’s not a special kind of model collapse that only happens in nonfiction and that this paper forgot to test for.
parent_text:
No. Cross-entropy loss is basically “can you correctly predict the next word in an actual text.”

If you can successfully do that 99% of the time, you’re not hallucinating 99% of the time. It’s just that we only call the ability “factuality” when it’s applied to nonfiction. +
parent_chain:
They’ve chosen to work on an example (TinyStories) that includes fiction, so low cross-entropy loss can only be interpreted as “coherence.” If they were working on Wikipedia the loss metric would also imply factuality (at least, according to Wikipedia).|||So then isn't the paper not relevant (doesn't provide evidence) one way or the other to whether synthetic data leads to "collapse" in the sense of "useless for search bc hallucinations"?|||No. Cross-entropy loss is basically “can you correctly predict the next word in an actual text.”

If you can successfully do that 99% of the time, you’re not hallucinating 99% of the time. It’s just that we only call the ability “factuality” when it’s applied to nonfiction. +

Of course it makes mistakes.  And over time those mistakes will lesson. 

But you can ask for its sources. You can review those sources. And you can question those sources to the model 

You can also take the output of one model and use it as input to another , and ask it questions

text: Model Collapse means that these models, when they start learning from themselves, are overrepresenting their own mistakes and assumptions in their datasets, which leads to more and more generalization until the generated content is indecipherable, basic and consistent.
parent_text: GenAI like GPT, Deepblue, Midjournet etc. are learning from other GenAI content - which to most people sounds like AI learning from itself, which would be a good thing. Unfortunately, for GenAI like these, that does not make them "true AI" whatsoever; it leads to model collapse.
parent_chain:

parent_text: Flagging: I don't really understand the precise distinction between weak and strong model collapse, which I think maps on to "tail collapse" and "model collapse" but I'm not sure.
parent_chain: I've had lots of thoughts about the model collapse and tail collapse thing without ever posting out it. I still haven't understood it to a satisfying degree, but I'm collect a bunch of links and posts here as a marker for future me.|||This original paper is cited a lot, but the understanding of the field has progressed a lot since then. I would need to dig into it more, but I think a lot of it is the replacement vs. accumulation and naive selection vs. pruned selection distinctions that really change the picture.|||On accumulation: "we extend this argument to prove that if data instead accumulate, the test error has a finite upper bound independent of the number of iterations, meaning model collapse no longer occurs"|||On selection: arxiv.org/html/2406.07...|||Flagging: I don't really understand the precise distinction between weak and strong model collapse, which I think maps on to "tail collapse" and "model collapse" but I'm not sure.
